threats.title: Systematic NAV Miscalculation via Inverted Token1 Premium Sign
threats.hypothesis_description: The computeNAV function incorrectly calculates token1 premium exposure by subtracting short premium from long premium, which is the inverse of the correct logic used for token0. This systematically distorts the vault's NAV.
threats.investigation_rationale: Accurate NAV is critical for fair share pricing during deposit and withdrawal fulfillments. An inverted sign means earned premiums reduce NAV and owed premiums increase NAV, allowing extraction of value by timing deposits/withdrawals.
preconditions: Vault has active positions with accumulated premiums in token1

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Asymmetric premium calculation logic between token0 and token1
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None for the calculation itself, though manager provides prices
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time NAV is computed for fulfillment while premiums exist

operational_questions: Is the inverted sign intentional due to Panoptic's internal accounting, or a bug?
How significantly can this skew the NAV in typical operational scenarios?
validation_requirements: Compare token0 and token1 premium definitions in Panoptic core contracts
Simulate NAV calculation with positive and negative token1 premiums

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe vault positions to identify large accumulated token1 premiums
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If vault owes premium (NAV artificially inflated), request withdrawal to extract excess assets
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If vault earned premium (NAV artificially deflated), request deposit to acquire cheap shares
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Wait for manager fulfillment to lock in the mispriced conversion
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires monitoring of Panoptic pool positions and timely requests

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Systematic mispricing of all user deposits and withdrawals
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires upgrading the accountant contract and recalculating historical losses

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - relies on normal protocol usage
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Must queue requests before manager fulfills epochs

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Discrepancies between actual pool value and reported NAV
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected drops or spikes in share price during fulfillments
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users depositing/withdrawing heavily when token1 premiums are large

threats.title: Artificial NAV Inflation via Per-Pool Debt Hiding
threats.hypothesis_description: The computeNAV function clamps each individual pool's exposure to zero before summing them. This hides underwater positions (debt) in specific pools, artificially inflating the aggregate NAV.
threats.investigation_rationale: If a vault operates across multiple pools, losses in one pool should offset gains in another. Clamping per pool means the vault appears more solvent than it is, overpaying withdrawers and diluting new depositors.
preconditions: Vault is configured with multiple pools
At least one pool has a net negative exposure (debt)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Clamping occurs inside the pool loop rather than on the final aggregate sum
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager must deploy capital across multiple pools
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever a pool position goes underwater

operational_questions: Can the manager intentionally structure positions to exploit this?
What happens when the debt in the underwater pool is eventually realized?
validation_requirements: Test NAV calculation with one profitable and one underwater pool
Verify if Panoptic allows cross-pool collateralization that makes this dangerous

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Manager or market conditions cause one pool to become underwater
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker observes inflated NAV and requests withdrawal
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Manager fulfills withdrawal, paying out assets based on inflated NAV
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Remaining shareholders absorb the hidden debt when positions are closed
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None for the attacker, just observation

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Vault insolvency as assets are drained faster than actual value supports
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - lost assets cannot be recovered from withdrawn users

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: During periods of divergent pool performance

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Vaults with multiple pools showing zero or negative exposure in some
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: NAV remaining stable despite obvious market losses in specific pools
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large withdrawals during partial market downturns

threats.title: Permanent Vault Lockout via Total Assets Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: If the vault's NAV drops below the sum of pending deposits and reserved withdrawals, the totalAssets calculation in fulfillment functions will underflow, permanently reverting all epoch fulfillments.
threats.investigation_rationale: Pending deposits and reserved withdrawals are not segregated from active investments. If the manager invests these funds and takes a loss, or if general NAV drops, the vault becomes completely bricked.
preconditions: NAV + 1 < depositEpochState[depositEpoch].assetsDeposited + reservedWithdrawalAssets

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Subtraction of liabilities from NAV without underflow protection
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager must incur losses or market must move against vault
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During market downturns or poor manager performance

operational_questions: Are pending deposits actually deployed by the manager before fulfillment?
Is there any recovery mechanism if this underflow occurs?
validation_requirements: Simulate a scenario where NAV drops significantly while deposits are pending
Check if cancelDeposit can reduce assetsDeposited enough to recover

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Users queue large deposits, increasing assetsDeposited
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Manager deploys vault capital (including pending funds) into positions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Positions take a loss, dropping NAV below the liability threshold
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Manager attempts to fulfill epochs but transactions revert due to underflow
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Relies on market conditions or manager error

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete halt of all vault deposits and withdrawals
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Extreme - requires manager to inject personal funds to raise NAV, or cancel deposits if possible

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Epochs remaining unfulfilled for extended periods
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Manager attempting to cancel deposits to restore solvency

threats.title: Systematic Share Loss via Double Rounding in executeDeposit
threats.hypothesis_description: Two consecutive mulDiv operations in executeDeposit compound rounding errors, systematically shorting users on shares and leaving dust permanently locked in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Financial math should minimize intermediate rounding. By rounding down twice (once for assets, once for shares), the protocol leaks value from users into unclaimable contract state.
preconditions: Partial fulfillment of a deposit epoch
Multiple users executing deposits

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Chained proportional calculations with integer division
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every time executeDeposit is called on a partially fulfilled epoch

operational_questions: How much value is lost to rounding in typical partial fulfillment scenarios?
Does the accumulated dust affect the totalSupply invariant?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact share loss across 100 users in a partially fulfilled epoch
Compare against a single-step calculation: mulDiv(queued, sharesReceived, assetsDeposited)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Manager partially fulfills a deposit epoch
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users call executeDeposit to claim their shares
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Each user loses a small amount of shares due to double rounding down
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Over time, totalSupply becomes inflated relative to actual user balances

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Slow erosion of user value and accounting inconsistencies
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible to recover lost dust shares

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Gap between totalSupply and sum of all user balances
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users receiving slightly fewer shares than expected

threats.title: Epoch Deadlock via Zero-Amount Fulfillment
threats.hypothesis_description: If the manager fulfills an epoch with zero assets or shares, the epoch state records zero for fulfilled amounts. Subsequent user executions for that epoch will revert due to division by zero, permanently locking their queued funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: There are no zero-checks in fulfillDeposits or fulfillWithdrawals. An operational mistake by the manager can create a poisoned epoch that traps all users who queued requests during that epoch.
preconditions: Manager calls fulfillDeposits with assetsToFulfill = 0 OR fulfillWithdrawals with sharesToFulfill = 0
Users have queued requests in that epoch

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing input validation for zero amounts in fulfillment functions
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager must execute the zero-amount fulfillment
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When manager attempts to advance epoch without fulfilling any amounts

operational_questions: Is it standard operational practice to advance epochs with zero fulfillment?
Can the manager rescue funds using cancelDeposit after the epoch has advanced?
validation_requirements: Test executeDeposit after a zero-amount fulfillDeposits
Verify if cancelDeposit works for past epochs (it doesn't, only current epoch)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Users queue deposits in epoch N
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Manager calls fulfillDeposits(0, ...) to advance to epoch N+1
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users call executeDeposit(user, N) to roll over their funds
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts due to division by zero on assetsFulfilled
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires manager error or malicious intent

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of access to user funds queued in the poisoned epoch

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users unable to execute their deposits/withdrawals

threats.title: Reentrancy in executeWithdrawal via Multi-Epoch Rollover
threats.hypothesis_description: executeWithdrawal rolls unfulfilled shares to the next epoch BEFORE making external token transfers. If the underlying token supports callbacks and the next epoch is already fulfilled, a reentrant call can execute the next epoch using the newly inflated rollover amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: State updates for future epochs occur before the CEI (Checks-Effects-Interactions) pattern completes. This allows an attacker to chain executions across epochs in a single transaction, potentially extracting more than their fair share of reserved assets.
deployment_assumptions: underlyingToken is an ERC777 or token with transfer callbacks (e.g., ERC677, ERC777)
threats.config_evidence: Vault assumes standard ERC20 but does not restrict token types
preconditions: Underlying token supports receiver callbacks
Epoch N is partially fulfilled
Epoch N+1 is already fulfilled by the manager
Attacker has unexecuted withdrawal in Epoch N

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State modification for epoch+1 before external call
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Manager must have fulfilled multiple epochs before user executes
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When multiple historical epochs are fulfilled but unexecuted by the user

operational_questions: Does the protocol intend to support tokens with callbacks?
Can the reentrant execution drain reservedWithdrawalAssets belonging to other users?
validation_requirements: Deploy with an ERC777 token and attempt reentrant executeWithdrawal
Calculate if the attacker receives more assets than intended

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker queues withdrawal in epoch N
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Manager partially fulfills epoch N, then fulfills epoch N+1
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker calls executeWithdrawal for epoch N
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: During token transfer callback, attacker reenters executeWithdrawal for epoch N+1
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires specific token and manager fulfillment timing

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Theft of reserved withdrawal assets, leaving other users unable to execute
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - stolen assets cannot be recovered

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Smart contract development for callback exploitation
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When multiple epochs are pending execution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple executeWithdrawal calls in the same transaction trace
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: reservedWithdrawalAssets depleted before all users claim

threats.title: Uninitialized Fee Wallet Blocks Profitable Withdrawals
threats.hypothesis_description: If the feeWallet is not set (remains address(0)) and the underlying token reverts on transfers to the zero address, all profitable withdrawals will revert during execution.
threats.investigation_rationale: The constructor does not initialize feeWallet, and there is no check in executeWithdrawal to skip the fee transfer if feeWallet is address(0). This creates a fragile deployment dependency.
deployment_assumptions: underlyingToken reverts on transfer to address(0)
threats.config_evidence: Constructor lacks feeWallet parameter; relies on setFeeWallet()
preconditions: feeWallet == address(0)
performanceFeeBps > 0
User withdrawal is profitable (assetsToWithdraw > withdrawnBasis)
Underlying token reverts on zero-address transfer

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Transfer to potentially uninitialized address without zero-check
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner must fail to call setFeeWallet
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From deployment until setFeeWallet is called

operational_questions: Is the deployment script guaranteed to set the feeWallet?
What happens to users who try to execute profitable withdrawals during this window?
validation_requirements: Check deployment scripts for setFeeWallet call
Test executeWithdrawal with address(0) feeWallet and standard ERC20

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Vault is deployed and operates normally
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users queue withdrawals, manager fulfills them
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users attempt to execute profitable withdrawals
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transactions revert, locking user funds until owner intervenes

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users cannot access their withdrawn funds
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - owner just needs to call setFeeWallet

threats.title: Vault Insolvency via Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The requestDeposit function credits the user with the requested 'assets' amount rather than the actual amount received by the vault. If a fee-on-transfer token is used, the vault will owe more assets than it actually holds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Standard vulnerability pattern for vaults. The discrepancy between recorded deposits and actual balances means the last users to withdraw will find the vault empty, effectively paying the transfer fees for early withdrawers.
threats.config_evidence: Vault assumes standard ERC20 behavior and does not check balance before/after transfer
preconditions: Underlying token applies a fee on transfer

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State update uses input parameter instead of actual transferred amount
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner must configure vault with a fee-on-transfer token

operational_questions: Does the protocol intend to support fee-on-transfer tokens?
How quickly does the vault become insolvent?
validation_requirements: Deploy vault with a mock fee-on-transfer token
Simulate multiple deposits and full withdrawals to observe insolvency

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Users deposit funds, vault records full amount but receives less
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Manager fulfills deposits and withdrawals based on recorded amounts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Early withdrawers receive full value, draining the vault's actual balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Late withdrawers are unable to execute as the vault lacks sufficient tokens

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Vault insolvency and loss of funds for late withdrawers

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Discrepancy between assetsDeposited and actual token balance
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed executeWithdrawal calls due to insufficient balance

threats.title: Manager Can Drain Reserved Withdrawal Assets
threats.hypothesis_description: The manage() function allows the manager to execute arbitrary calls, including transferring out the underlying tokens that back reservedWithdrawalAssets. This breaks the invariant that fulfilled withdrawals are guaranteed to be executable.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the manager is trusted to deploy capital, reserved assets belong to users whose withdrawals have already been fulfilled. Allowing the manager to drain these assets violates the core segregation of user claims from active vault capital.
preconditions: Manager acts maliciously or is compromised
Vault holds underlying tokens for reservedWithdrawalAssets

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Arbitrary execution capability with no balance checks on reserved assets
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after withdrawals are fulfilled but before users execute them

operational_questions: Is the manager expected to move underlying tokens manually, or only interact with Panoptic pools?
Should there be a balance check after manage() to ensure reserved assets remain?
validation_requirements: Verify if manage() can transfer underlyingToken
Confirm that executeWithdrawal fails if balance is drained

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Manager fulfills large withdrawals, increasing reservedWithdrawalAssets
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Before users call executeWithdrawal, manager calls manage() to transfer underlying tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Tokens are sent to an attacker-controlled address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users attempt to execute withdrawals but transactions revert

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible without returning the stolen funds

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: manage() calls targeting the underlyingToken contract
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Unexpected token transfers out of the vault

threats.title: Irreversible Vault Lock Freezes NAV Updates
threats.hypothesis_description: The lockVault function in PanopticVaultAccountant is irreversible. If an incorrect pool configuration is locked, or if pools need upgrading, the vault's NAV computation is permanently frozen to the locked configuration.
threats.investigation_rationale: Operational mistakes happen. Providing a one-way lock without an unlock mechanism means a simple configuration error by the owner permanently bricks the vault's ability to accurately price shares.
deployment_assumptions: Owner mistakenly locks the vault with incorrect pools
Or protocol upgrades require pool changes after locking
threats.config_evidence: lockVault() sets vaultLocked[vault] = true with no function to set it to false
preconditions: Owner calls lockVault
Pool configuration needs to be changed subsequently

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: One-way state transition for critical configuration

operational_questions: Is the lock intended to be permanent to provide trust guarantees to users?
What is the operational recovery procedure if a locked pool is deprecated?
validation_requirements: Verify absence of unlock mechanism
Check if vault owner can simply set a new accountant contract to bypass the lock

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: A pool in the configuration becomes deprecated or compromised
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Owner attempts to update the pool hash but is blocked by vaultLocked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Vault NAV becomes inaccurate, halting safe fulfillments
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires operational error or external protocol changes

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inability to update NAV calculation logic
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires vault owner to deploy and set a new accountant contract
